full_context: According to GB/T 15543-2008 "Power quality - Three-phase voltage unbalance", Chapter 4, the relationship between rated voltage, rated current and rated capacity in power systems is expressed by Formula (4.1): U_n = S/(√3 I_n). Where: U_n is rated line voltage, kV; S is rated capacity, MVA; I_n is rated line current, kA. This is a fundamental calculation formula for power systems.

variable_definitions.name: Rated Line Voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard voltage for power system design and operation

variable_definitions.name: Rated Capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard capacity for electrical equipment design and operation

variable_definitions.name: Rated Line Current
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard current for electrical equipment design and operation

potential_questions: Given a transformer rated capacity S=50 MVA, rated current I_n=1.44 kA, calculate rated line voltage U_n.
task_description: 1. Obtain S and I_n; 2. Calculate √3; 3. Calculate U_n = S/(√3 × I_n); 4. Output U_n.

full_context: According to GB/T 12325-2008 "Power quality - Supply voltage deviation", Appendix A, voltage drop in a line is approximately calculated by Formula (A.1): ΔU = (PR + QX)/U_n. Where: ΔU is voltage drop, V; P is active power, kW; Q is reactive power, kvar; R is line resistance, Ω; X is line reactance, Ω; U_n is rated voltage, kV. Used for voltage quality analysis in distribution networks.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage Drop
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage difference between sending and receiving ends

variable_definitions.name: Active Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Power component that does actual work

variable_definitions.name: Reactive Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Power component for establishing electromagnetic field

potential_questions: Given 10 kV line: P=800 kW, Q=600 kvar, R=0.5 Ω, X=0.8 Ω, calculate voltage drop ΔU.
task_description: 1. Obtain P, Q, R, X, U_n; 2. Calculate PR; 3. Calculate QX; 4. Calculate PR+QX; 5. Calculate ΔU = (PR+QX)/U_n; 6. Output ΔU.

full_context: According to GB/T 15945-2008 "Power quality - Frequency deviation of power system", Chapter 5, the relationship between frequency, pole pairs and speed of synchronous generator is expressed by Formula (5.1): f = pn/60. Where: f is frequency, Hz; p is number of pole pairs; n is rotational speed, r/min. This is the basic operating principle of synchronous machines.

variable_definitions.name: Frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of AC cycles per second

variable_definitions.name: Number of Pole Pairs
variable_definitions.meaning: Pairs of magnetic poles in generator

potential_questions: Given hydro-generator pole pairs p=20, speed n=150 r/min, calculate frequency f.
task_description: 1. Obtain p and n; 2. Calculate f = (p × n)/60; 3. Output f.

full_context: According to GB/T 17626-2006 "Electromagnetic compatibility - Testing and measurement techniques", Appendix B, total harmonic distortion of voltage is calculated by Formula (B.1): THD = √[Σ(U_h/U₁)²]. Where: THD is total harmonic distortion, %; U_h is RMS voltage of h-th harmonic, V; U₁ is RMS fundamental voltage, V. Used for power quality harmonic analysis.

variable_definitions.name: Total Harmonic Distortion
variable_definitions.meaning: Square root of sum of squared ratios of harmonic to fundamental voltages

variable_definitions.name: Harmonic Voltage RMS
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value of h-th harmonic voltage component

variable_definitions.name: Fundamental Voltage RMS
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value of 50 Hz fundamental component

potential_questions: Given voltages: fundamental U₁=220 V, 3rd U₃=8.8 V, 5th U₅=6.6 V, 7th U₇=4.4 V, calculate THD.
task_description: 1. Obtain U₁ and harmonic voltages; 2. Calculate (U₃/U₁)²; 3. Calculate (U₅/U₁)²; 4. Calculate (U₇/U₁)²; 5. Sum the three values; 6. Calculate THD = √(sum); 7. Output THD.

full_context: According to GB/T 50062-2008 "Design code for relay protection and automation of power installations", Chapter 3, RMS periodic component of three-phase short-circuit current is calculated by Formula (3.1): I_k⁽³⁾ = U_av/(√3 Z_Σ). Where: I_k⁽³⁾ is three-phase short-circuit current, kA; U_av is average rated voltage at fault point, kV; Z_Σ is total impedance of short-circuit loop, Ω. Used for power system short-circuit calculation.

variable_definitions.name: Three-Phase Short-Circuit Current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS periodic component under three-phase fault

variable_definitions.name: Average Rated Voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Average voltage of voltage level at fault point

variable_definitions.name: Total Impedance
variable_definitions.meaning: Total impedance from source to fault point

potential_questions: Given 10.5 kV bus fault, total impedance Z_Σ=0.8 Ω, calculate three-phase short-circuit current I_k⁽³⁾.
task_description: 1. Obtain U_av and Z_Σ; 2. Calculate √3; 3. Calculate I_k⁽³⁾ = U_av/(√3 × Z_Σ); 4. Output I_k⁽³⁾.

full_context: According to GB/T 50063-2017 "Design code for electrical measuring instruments of power installations", Chapter 4, transformer capacity selection is calculated by Formula (4.1): S_t = P/cosφ. Where: S_t is transformer rated capacity, kVA; P is calculated active power, kW; cosφ is power factor. Used for determining transformer capacity.

variable_definitions.name: Power Factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of active power to apparent power

potential_questions: Given workshop active power P=800 kW, power factor cosφ=0.85, calculate required transformer capacity S_t.
task_description: 1. Obtain P and cosφ; 2. Calculate S_t = P/cosφ; 3. Output S_t.

full_context: According to GB/T 50064-2014 "Design code for overvoltage protection and insulation coordination of AC electrical installations", Appendix C, reactive compensation capacity is calculated by Formula (C.1): Q_c = P(tanφ₁ − tanφ₂). Where: Q_c is compensation capacity, kvar; P is active power, kW; φ₁, φ₂ are power factor angles before and after compensation. Used for power factor correction.
potential_questions: Given P=1000 kW, cosφ₁=0.75, target cosφ₂=0.95, calculate compensation capacity Q_c.
task_description: 1. Obtain P, cosφ₁, cosφ₂; 2. Calculate φ₁ = arccos(cosφ₁); 3. Calculate φ₂ = arccos(cosφ₂); 4. Calculate tanφ₁ and tanφ₂; 5. Calculate Q_c = P × (tanφ₁ − tanφ₂); 6. Output Q_c.

full_context: According to GB/T 50065-2011 "Design code for earthing of AC electrical installations", Chapter 5, earthing resistance of vertical earth electrode is calculated by Formula (5.1): R_E = ρ/(2πL) · ln(4L/d). Where: R_E is earthing resistance, Ω; ρ is soil resistivity, Ω·m; L is length of earth electrode, m; d is diameter of earth electrode, m. Commonly used in earthing design.

variable_definitions.name: Electrode Length
variable_definitions.meaning: Buried length of vertical earth electrode

potential_questions: Given ρ=200 Ω·m, L=2.5 m, d=0.05 m, calculate earthing resistance R_E.
task_description: 1. Obtain ρ, L, d; 2. Calculate 4L/d; 3. Calculate ln(4L/d); 4. Calculate R_E = ρ/(2πL) × ln(4L/d); 5. Output R_E.

full_context: According to GB/T 16895-2017 "Electrical installations in buildings", Chapter 52, design current of three-phase balanced load is calculated by Formula (52.1): I_b = P/(√3 U cosφ). Where: I_b is design current, A; P is equipment power, W; U is line voltage, V; cosφ is power factor. Used for cable and switch selection.

variable_definitions.name: Design Current
variable_definitions.meaning: Current for equipment selection and verification

variable_definitions.name: Equipment Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated active power of electrical equipment

potential_questions: Given three-phase motor P=15 kW, U=380 V, cosφ=0.85, calculate design current I_b.
task_description: 1. Obtain P, U, cosφ; 2. Convert P to W; 3. Calculate √3; 4. Calculate I_b = P/(√3 × U × cosφ); 5. Output I_b.

full_context: According to GB/T 2099-2008 "Plugs and socket-outlets for household and similar purposes", Appendix A, thermal resistance of semiconductor device is calculated by Formula (A.1): R_th = (T_j − T_a)/P. Where: R_th is thermal resistance, K/W; T_j is junction temperature, K; T_a is ambient temperature, K; P is power dissipation, W. Used for thermal analysis of electronic devices.

variable_definitions.name: Junction Temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum operating temperature of semiconductor chip

potential_questions: Given T_j=125 °C, T_a=25 °C, P=10 W, calculate thermal resistance R_th.
task_description: 1. Obtain T_j, T_a, P; 2. Use consistent temperature units; 3. Calculate ΔT = T_j − T_a; 4. Calculate R_th = ΔT/P; 5. Output R_th.

full_context: According to GB/T 4728-2008 "Graphical symbols for electrical diagrams", Chapter 2, output voltage of resistive voltage divider is calculated by Formula (2.1): V_out = V_in · R₂/(R₁+R₂). Where: V_out is output voltage, V; V_in is input voltage, V; R₁, R₂ are divider resistors, Ω. Basic formula for circuit analysis.
potential_questions: Given V_in=12 V, R₁=4 kΩ, R₂=6 kΩ, calculate output voltage V_out.
task_description: 1. Obtain V_in, R₁, R₂; 2. Calculate R₁+R₂; 3. Calculate R₂/(R₁+R₂); 4. Calculate V_out = V_in × R₂/(R₁+R₂); 5. Output V_out.

full_context: According to GB/T 4793-2007 "Safety requirements for electrical equipment for measurement, control and laboratory use", Appendix B, time constant of RC circuit is calculated by Formula (B.1): τ = RC. Where: τ is time constant, s; R is resistance, Ω; C is capacitance, F. Determines charging and discharging speed.
potential_questions: Given R=10 kΩ, C=100 μF, calculate time constant τ.
task_description: 1. Obtain R and C; 2. Convert to Ω and F; 3. Calculate τ = R × C; 4. Output τ.

full_context: According to GB/T 4857-2005 "Packaging - Basic tests for transport packages", Chapter 3, resonant frequency of LC circuit is calculated by Formula (3.1): f = 1/(2π√(LC)). Where: f is resonant frequency, Hz; L is inductance, H; C is capacitance, F. Used in filter and oscillator design.

variable_definitions.name: Resonant Frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Frequency at which LC circuit resonates

potential_questions: Given L=10 mH, C=0.1 μF, calculate resonant frequency f.
task_description: 1. Obtain L and C; 2. Convert to H and F; 3. Calculate L×C; 4. Calculate √(LC); 5. Calculate 2π√(LC); 6. Calculate f = 1/(2π√(LC)); 7. Output f.

full_context: According to GB/T 5465-2008 "Graphical symbols for electrical equipment", Appendix C, impedance of series RL circuit is calculated by Formula (C.1): Z = √(R² + (ωL)²). Where: Z is impedance, Ω; R is resistance, Ω; ω is angular frequency, rad/s; L is inductance, H. Used for AC circuit analysis.

variable_definitions.name: Impedance
variable_definitions.meaning: Complex opposition to current in AC circuit

variable_definitions.name: Angular Frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Rate of change of AC in rad/s

potential_questions: Given R=100 Ω, L=0.1 H, f=50 Hz, calculate impedance Z.
task_description: 1. Obtain R, L, f; 2. Calculate ω = 2πf; 3. Calculate (ωL)²; 4. Calculate R²; 5. Calculate R² + (ωL)²; 6. Calculate Z = √(R² + (ωL)²); 7. Output Z.

full_context: According to GB/T 7153-2008 "Directly heated cathode electronic tubes", Chapter 4, collector current of BJT is related to base current by Formula (4.1): I_C = β I_B. Where: I_C is collector current, A; β is current gain; I_B is base current, A. Core of transistor amplification principle.

variable_definitions.name: Current Gain
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of collector current to base current

potential_questions: Given β=100, I_B=20 μA, calculate collector current I_C.
task_description: 1. Obtain β and I_B; 2. Convert I_B to A; 3. Calculate I_C = β × I_B; 4. Output I_C.

full_context: According to GB/T 7676-2017 "Direct acting indicating analogue electrical measuring instruments and their accessories", Appendix D, Ohm’s law is expressed by Formula (D.1): V = IR. Where: V is voltage, V; I is current, A; R is resistance, Ω. Fundamental law of circuit analysis.
potential_questions: Given R=1 kΩ, I=5 mA, calculate voltage V across resistor.
task_description: 1. Obtain I and R; 2. Convert to A and Ω; 3. Calculate V = I × R; 4. Output V.

full_context: According to GB/T 11021-2014 "Electrical insulating materials - Thermal endurance properties", Chapter 3, heating power of resistor is calculated by Joule’s law: P = I²R. Where: P is heating power, W; I is current through resistor, A; R is resistance, Ω. Used for thermal design of electrical equipment.
potential_questions: Given R=10 Ω, I=2 A, calculate heating power P.
task_description: 1. Obtain I and R; 2. Calculate I²; 3. Calculate P = I² × R; 4. Output P.

full_context: According to GB/T 14048-2012 "Low-voltage switchgear and controlgear", Appendix E, apparent power is related to active and reactive power by Formula (E.1): S = √(P² + Q²). Where: S is apparent power, VA; P is active power, W; Q is reactive power, var. Basic power triangle relationship.

variable_definitions.name: Apparent Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of RMS voltage and RMS current

potential_questions: Given P=800 W, Q=600 var, calculate apparent power S.
task_description: 1. Obtain P and Q; 2. Calculate P²; 3. Calculate Q²; 4. Calculate P²+Q²; 5. Calculate S = √(P²+Q²); 6. Output S.

full_context: According to GB/T 14549-1993 "Power quality - Harmonics in public supply network", Chapter 4, power factor is calculated by Formula (4.1): cosφ = P/S. Where: cosφ is power factor; P is active power, kW; S is apparent power, kVA. Used for power quality assessment.
potential_questions: Given P=500 kW, S=625 kVA, calculate power factor cosφ.
task_description: 1. Obtain P and S; 2. Calculate cosφ = P/S; 3. Output cosφ.

full_context: According to GB/T 15284-2002 "Rotating electrical machines - Rating and performance", Appendix A, efficiency of electric machine is calculated by Formula (A.1): η = P_out/P_in. Where: η is efficiency; P_out is output power, kW; P_in is input power, kW. Important performance indicator.

variable_definitions.name: Efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of output power to input power

potential_questions: Given P_in=55 kW, P_out=50 kW, calculate efficiency η.
task_description: 1. Obtain P_in and P_out; 2. Calculate η = P_out/P_in; 3. Output η.

full_context: According to GB/T 16927-2011 "High-voltage test techniques", Chapter 3, RMS value of AC voltage is defined by Formula (3.1): U_rms = √[(1/T)∫₀^T u²(t)dt]. Where: U_rms is RMS voltage, V; T is period, s; u(t) is instantaneous voltage, V. Basis of AC measurement.

variable_definitions.name: RMS Voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: DC equivalent of AC for thermal effect

variable_definitions.name: Period
variable_definitions.meaning: Time for one full AC cycle

potential_questions: Given u(t)=311 sin(314t) V, calculate RMS voltage U_rms.
task_description: 1. Identify sine form u(t)=U_m sin(ωt); 2. RMS = U_m/√2; 3. Substitute U_m=311 V; 4. Calculate U_rms = 311/√2; 5. Output U_rms.
